title: Robust error handling for all commands

story.i_want_to: see clear, helpful error messages when I make a mistake
story.so_that: I know what went wrong and how to fix it instead of seeing a Python traceback

rationale: Unhandled exceptions and cryptic tracebacks make a tool feel broken and unprofessional. Good error messages turn mistakes into learning moments and keep users confident in the tool.

acceptance_criteria.description: Passing a non-numeric ID to done/delete/edit prints 'Error: Task ID must be a positive number.' to stderr and exits with code 1.
Passing zero or a negative ID to done/delete/edit prints 'Error: Task ID must be a positive number.' to stderr and exits with code 1.
If tasks.json contains invalid JSON, the app prints 'Error: Task file is corrupted. Please check ~/.todo/tasks.json' to stderr and exits with code 1.
No Python tracebacks are ever shown to the user under normal usage.
All error messages are printed to stderr (not stdout) and follow the format 'Error: <message>'.
Missing argument errors include the correct usage hint on the next line (e.g., 'Error: Please provide a task description.\nUsage: todo add <description>').
If the tasks file or its directory cannot be read or written due to permissions, the app prints 'Error: Cannot access ~/.todo/tasks.json — check file permissions.' to stderr and exits with code 1.
If writing the tasks file fails due to disk full or I/O error, the app prints 'Error: Could not save tasks. Check disk space and permissions.' to stderr and exits with code 1.

Running an unknown command (e.g., `todo foobar`) prints 'Error: Unknown command "foobar". Run `todo help` for usage.' to stderr and exits with code 1. If the command is within edit distance 2 of a known command, suggests it.
The entry point catches all unexpected exceptions and prints 'Error: An unexpected error occurred: {message}' to stderr and exits with code 1. KeyboardInterrupt exits silently with code 130.
Extra unexpected arguments to commands are silently ignored (e.g., `todo done 3 extra junk` processes ID 3 only).

test_cases.name: Non-numeric ID shows helpful error
steps: Run `python todo.py done abc` with HOME=tmp_dir
expected_results: stderr contains 'Error: Task ID must be a positive number.'

test_cases.name: Corrupted JSON file shows helpful error
preconditions: tmp_dir with tasks.json containing invalid JSON (e.g., '{broken')

test_cases.name: Missing description for add shows usage hint
Missing ID for delete shows usage hint
Missing ID for done shows usage hint
expected_results: stderr contains 'Error: Please provide a task ID.'

test_cases.name: Missing arguments for edit shows usage hint
expected_results: First: stderr contains 'Error' and 'Usage: todo edit <id> <description>'
Second: stderr contains 'Error' and 'Usage: todo edit <id> <description>'

test_cases.name: Zero and negative IDs show error
expected_results: Both stderr contain 'Error: Task ID must be a positive number.'

test_cases.name: Unknown command shows error with suggestion
expected_results: Second: stderr contains 'Did you mean: list?'

test_cases.name: Permission error shows user-friendly message
preconditions: tmp_dir with tasks.json set to read-only (chmod 444)

technical_notes: Wrap main() in try/except to catch unexpected errors gracefully
Use print(..., file=sys.stderr) for error output
Validate ID conversion with try/except ValueError around int(), then check > 0
Catch PermissionError and OSError for file system errors
Each command handler should validate its own required arguments
All error messages follow format: 'Error: <message>' optionally followed by 'Usage: ...'
Catch KeyboardInterrupt at the top level; exit with sys.exit(130)
